papers.title: High-velocity stars in the cores of globular clusters: The illustrative case of NGC 2808
papers.author_names: N. Lutzgendorf A. Gualandris M. Kissler-Patig K. Gebhardt H. Baumgardt E. Noyola J. Kruijssen B. Jalali P. Zeeuw N. Neumayer

abstract.text: We report the detection of five high-velocity stars in the core of the globular cluster NGC 2808. The stars lie on the the red giant branch and show total velocities between 40 and 45 km/s. For a core velocity dispersion sigma_c = 13.4 km/s, this corresponds to up to 3.4 sigma_c. These velocities are close to the estimated escape velocity (~ 50 km/s) and suggest an ejection from the core. Two of these stars have been confirmed in our recent integral field spectroscopy data and we will discuss them in more detail here. These two red giants are located at a projected distance of ~ 0.3 pc from the center. According to their positions on the color magnitude diagram, both stars are cluster members. We investigate several possible origins for the high velocities of the stars and conceivable ejection mechanisms. Since the velocities are close to the escape velocity, it is not obvious whether the stars are bound or unbound to the cluster. We therefore consider both cases in our analysis. We perform numerical simulations of three-body dynamical encounters between binaries and single stars and compare the resulting velocity distributions of escapers with the velocities of our stars. We compare the predictions for a single dynamical encounter with a compact object with those of a sequence of two-body encounters due to relaxation. If the stars are unbound, the encounter must have taken place recently, when the stars were already in the giant phase. After including binary fractions and black-hole retention fractions, projection effects, and detection probabilities from Monte-Carlo simulations, we estimate the expected numbers of detections for all the different scenarios. Based on these numbers, we conclude that the most likely scenario is that the stars are bound and were accelerated by a single encounter between a binary of main-sequence stars and a ~ 10 M_sun black hole.

triage.label: rule-direct
triage.reason: Direct title match: high-velocity or extreme-velocity stars

brief.tldr: [research paper] The paper reports the detection of five high-velocity red giant stars in the core of the globular cluster NGC 2808, with velocities up to 3.4σ_c, suggesting ejection from the core. Through Monte-Carlo simulations of three-body dynamical encounters, it finds that the most likely origin is a single dynamical interaction between a main-sequence binary and a ∼10 M⊙ black hole, occurring when the stars were on the main sequence. The analysis supports that such ejections are common in globular clusters and can explain fast stars in M3 and 47 Tucanae via similar dynamical mechanisms.

source_fields.title: High-velocity stars in the cores of globular clusters: The illustrative case of NGC 2808

source_fields.sections_text: Introduction: This paper presents the first detailed kinematic and photometric study of high-velocity stars in NGC 2808, identifying and characterizing five such stars—including two with velocities exceeding 4 times the core velocity dispersion—that provide new observational evidence supporting the presence of an intermediate-mass black hole in the cluster's center. Radial velocities: Radial velocity measurements from multiple spectroscopic datasets, combined with proper motions for four of the five fast stars in NGC 2808, identify five high-velocity stars in the cluster core, with FS1 and FS2 being the most prominent and confirmed through both IFU and Fabry-Perot observations. Dynamical encounters: This section investigates whether dynamical three-body encounters during the main-sequence stage can accelerate main-sequence stars to observed high velocities, through numerical simulations of binary-single star interactions involving various compact objects. Possible explanations: The high velocities of the stars FS1 and FS2 are unlikely to be due to foreground contamination, as their positions on the color-magnitude diagram strongly suggest cluster membership, despite the possibility of distant foreground stars with extreme distances. Summary and conclusions: The five high-velocity stars in NGC 2808 are likely bound cluster members accelerated by three-body scattering events involving main-sequence stars and a ∼10 M☉ stellar-mass black hole at the cluster center, without requiring an intermediate-mass black hole. Comparison with other globular clusters: This section compares high-velocity stars in globular clusters like 47 Tuc, M3, and NGC 6752, concluding that dynamical encounters with massive objects—such as black holes—during the main-sequence phase are a plausible origin for these stars, given similar central densities and velocity dispersions, and supported by the presence of off-center pulsars indicating central massive objects. The bound case - Maxwellian distribution: The section evaluates the likelihood that fast stars in a cluster acquired their high velocities through uncorrelated two-body encounters via relaxation, concluding that such events are extremely rare due to the low probability of stars exceeding 3.5σ in a Maxwellian velocity distribution and being detected in observational data.

abstract: We report the detection of five high-velocity stars in the core of the globular cluster NGC 2808. The stars lie on the the red giant branch and show total velocities between 40 and 45 km/s.
According to their positions on the color magnitude diagram, both stars are cluster members.
We perform numerical simulations of three-body dynamical encounters between binaries and single stars and compare the resulting velocity distributions of escapers with the velocities of our stars.
If the stars are unbound, the encounter must have taken place recently, when the stars were already in the giant phase.

Based on these numbers, we conclude that the most likely scenario is that the stars are bound and were accelerated by a single encounter between a binary of main-sequence stars and a ~ 10 M_sun black hole
title: High-velocity stars in the cores of globular clusters: The illustrative case of NGC 2808

catalog_assessment.evidence: Paper reports detection of five specific high-velocity stars in NGC 2808 with measured velocities, positions, and follow-up spectroscopy confirming two stars. Contains observational data on individual objects.
